instruction: Why does CPython use reference counting plus a separate cyclic garbage collector? Explain in exactly three short paragraphs. Do not use bullet points and do not mention any non-CPython implementation.

response.text:
CPython increments and decrements a per-object refcount on every assignment, function call, and scope exit. The moment a refcount hits zero the object is freed deterministically, which keeps memory pressure predictable and lets `__del__` run promptly. This is cheap for the common case and is the reason simple scripts rarely need a GC at all.

Refcounting alone cannot reclaim reference cycles, though. A list that contains itself, or two objects that hold each other through attributes, will never drop to zero even after the program forgets about them. That memory would leak for the life of the process, which is unacceptable for long-running services.

The cyclic collector closes that hole. It periodically walks tracked container objects, decrements refs that come from inside the candidate set, and reclaims any group whose external refcount is zero. It runs in generations so most short-lived objects never even reach it, which keeps the steady-state cost low.

instruction: explain csrf vs ssrf like im a junior dev. keep it under 200 words, no bullet lists, and dont just say 'one is server one is client'

response.text:
CSRF tricks an authenticated user's browser into making a request the user did not intend. The attacker hosts a page that quietly submits a form to bank.example.com; the victim's cookies ride along because the browser attaches them automatically. Mitigation lives client-side-ish: same-site cookies, CSRF tokens that the attacker page cannot read, and re-authentication on dangerous actions.

SSRF tricks the server itself into making a request the operator did not intend. You upload a profile-picture URL of `http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/` and the server, which has cloud credentials in its environment, happily fetches it and hands you back the response. Mitigation lives server-side: deny RFC1918 and link-local ranges, resolve DNS once and pin the IP, and never let user-supplied URLs reach the internal network.

The useful mental model: CSRF abuses what the user is allowed to do, SSRF abuses what the server is allowed to reach.

instruction: What does `await` actually do in JavaScript? Answer with exactly one paragraph, then one short code example. Do not use the words 'magic' or 'pause'.
whats teh diff between TCP keepalive and HTTP keepalive. im confused. dont give me an rfc dump, just like 4-5 sentences

response.text: They live at different layers and solve different problems. TCP keepalive is a kernel-level probe that fires after a long idle period (default 2 hours on Linux) to detect a peer that disappeared without sending FIN; if no ACK comes back, the socket is torn down. HTTP keepalive (a.k.a. persistent connections) is an application-layer choice to send multiple HTTP requests over the same already-open TCP connection instead of dialing a new one per request. So TCP keepalive answers 'is this socket still alive?', HTTP keepalive answers 'should I reuse this socket for the next request?'. They are orthogonal: you can have HTTP keepalive on with TCP keepalive off, and vice versa.

instruction: Why is Postgres' MVCC said to make VACUUM mandatory? Answer in exactly two paragraphs. Do not mention any other database engine.

response.text:
Postgres never updates a row in place. An UPDATE writes a new tuple version with a new xmin, and the old tuple gets an xmax marking when it became invisible. Readers at older snapshots can still see the old version, which is what gives Postgres its lock-free reads. The cost is that every UPDATE and DELETE leaves a dead tuple sitting in the heap and in every index that pointed at it.

Nothing in the write path reclaims those dead tuples; the table just grows. VACUUM is the process that walks the heap, marks dead tuples as free space, and updates the visibility map and the free-space map so future inserts can reuse the holes. Without it, table bloat, index bloat, and eventually transaction-ID wraparound will degrade the database. Autovacuum makes this automatic but it is still 'mandatory' in the sense that disabling it is a way to lose a production database.

instruction: explain what 'pinned dependency' actually means in a python project. dont use the phrase 'reproducible builds'. keep it tight, 3 sentences max.

response.text: A pinned dependency is one whose exact version (and ideally hash) is recorded so that two installations of the same project resolve to the same artifact, no matter when or where they run. `requests>=2.0` is not pinned; `requests==2.32.3 --hash=sha256:...` is. The pin lives in a lock file (e.g. `requirements.txt` written by `pip-compile`, `uv.lock`, `poetry.lock`) — your top-level `pyproject.toml` typically stays loose so humans can read intent, while the lock file is what the deploy actually installs from.
